questions.prompt: Which controls together defend against the domain hijack chain? (Select all that apply.)
choices: DNSSEC signing at the DNS host plus DS record at the registrar
CAA records limiting which CAs can issue certs
questions.explanation: Each layer covers a different attacker step: MFA stops account takeover, transfer lock stops registrar transfer, DNSSEC stops in-transit answer tampering, CAA stops rogue cert issuance. Disabling DMARC weakens defence; it's not a hijack control.

questions.prompt: DNSSEC was enabled at the DNS host but the customer's domain still doesn't validate. What's the most likely missing step?
choices: DNSSEC needs DMARC at p=reject first
The DS record was not published at the registrar to link the chain
DNSSEC requires CAA records to be set
questions.explanation: DNSSEC has two halves: signing at the DNS host, and the DS record in the parent zone (set at the registrar). Without the DS record, validating resolvers can't verify the signature chain and treat the answers as unsigned.
